2
Chapter 02  ·  Statistical Inference

Maximum Likelihood
Estimation

From the likelihood function to large-sample theory — the most powerful and widely used framework for parameter estimation

Learning Objectives

  • Construct the likelihood function for any parametric model and interpret it geometrically
  • Derive the MLE analytically via the score equation and numerically via optimization
  • Contrast MLE with the Method of Moments — when they agree and when they differ
  • Define and verify unbiasedness, consistency, and efficiency for estimators
  • Understand the bias–variance tradeoff: why biased estimators can be superior in practice
  • Apply the invariance principle to transform MLEs without re-deriving them
  • Derive MLEs for multinomial cell probabilities and understand the connection to contingency tables
  • State and apply the large-sample theory of MLE: asymptotic normality and the Fisher information
  • Construct confidence intervals from the MLE using the asymptotic normal approximation

2.1 Point Estimation — The Setup

In Chapter 1 we established the basic problem: we observe a sample $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} F_\theta$ where the parameter $\theta$ is unknown. Now we face the question head-on: how do we produce a specific numerical guess for $\theta$?

This is the problem of point estimation. A point estimator is a single-number summary of the data that we hope is "close" to $\theta$. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is a point estimator of the population mean $\mu$. The sample variance $S^2$ is a point estimator of $\sigma^2$. But the real challenge is: how do we systematically derive good estimators for arbitrary parameters, without having to rely on intuition case by case?

Two principled methods dominate statistics and machine learning: the Method of Moments and Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Both reduce estimation to a systematic recipe. MLE is more powerful, more general, and underpins virtually all modern statistical modeling — from logistic regression to deep learning. We develop both, then spend the rest of the chapter on MLE.

2.1.1 What Is a Point Estimator?

Definition · Point Estimator

A point estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ of a parameter $\theta$ is any function $\hat{\theta}_n = T(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ of the sample. It is a random variable — it varies across different samples — and its realization on a particular dataset is called the point estimate.

We measure how good an estimator is via its mean squared error: $$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}_n) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta)^2\bigr]$$ which decomposes into two fundamental components: $$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}_n) = \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}_n] - \theta\bigr)^2}_{\text{bias}^2} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_n)}_{\text{variance}}$$ This is the bias–variance decomposition, and we will return to it in Section 2.3.4.

Notice the structure: an estimator can fail in two distinct ways. It can be systematically wrong (biased) even with infinite data, or it can be noisy (high variance) even if it is right on average. A good estimator keeps both terms small — but as we will see, there is an irreducible tension between them.

2.1.2 Method of Moments

The Method of Moments (MoM) is the oldest estimation technique. It is based on a simple idea: the population moments — $\mu_k = \mathbb{E}[X^k]$ — are functions of $\theta$. The corresponding sample moments — $\hat{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i^k$ — estimate them consistently. So: set sample moments equal to population moments, and solve for $\theta$.

Definition · Method of Moments Estimator

Suppose the model has $p$ unknown parameters $\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)$. Write the first $p$ population moments as functions of $\theta$: $$\mu_k(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\theta[X^k], \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$ The method of moments estimator $\hat{\theta}^{\text{MoM}}$ is the solution to the system: $$\mu_k(\hat{\theta}) = \hat{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i^k, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$

Example · MoM for a Normal Distribution

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. We have two parameters, so we use two moments:

  • First moment: $\mu_1(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[X] = \mu$
  • Second moment: $\mu_2(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] = \sigma^2 + \mu^2$

Setting sample moments equal to population moments:

  • $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$
  • $\hat{\sigma}^2 + \hat{\mu}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2$, which gives $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2 - \bar{X}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$

The MoM estimator of $\sigma^2$ divides by $n$, not $n-1$. This is the biased version — as we showed in Chapter 1. The MoM does not automatically produce unbiased estimators.

Example · MoM for a Gamma Distribution

Let $X_i \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ with mean $\alpha/\beta$ and variance $\alpha/\beta^2$. Setting: $$\frac{\hat\alpha}{\hat\beta} = \bar{X}, \qquad \frac{\hat\alpha}{\hat\beta^2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = \hat\sigma^2$$ Dividing the first by the second: $\hat\beta = \bar{X}/\hat\sigma^2$. Then $\hat\alpha = \bar{X}^2/\hat\sigma^2$. The MoM gives a closed-form answer in one step — which MLE for the Gamma distribution does not.

Method of Moments is easy to compute and often gives consistent estimators. Its weakness: it ignores information in the shape of the distribution. If the data are highly non-normal, MoM may be far from optimal, and the resulting estimators can have large variance. MLE fixes this by directly using the full distributional model.

2.1.3 Introducing Maximum Likelihood

The core philosophy of MLE is simple and natural: choose the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable. If you observed the data $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$, then the MLE $\hat{\theta}$ is the value of $\theta$ under which $\mathbf{x}$ was most likely to occur.

This principle was developed and championed by Ronald A. Fisher in the 1920s. It has since become the bedrock of parametric statistical inference, maximum a posteriori estimation in Bayesian statistics, and the loss functions of the most widely used machine learning models (cross-entropy loss is a negative log-likelihood).

💡
Key insight: When you minimize cross-entropy loss in logistic regression or neural network training, you are maximizing the log-likelihood of the data under a Bernoulli model. MLE is not a niche statistical tool — it is the foundational justification for the loss functions you use every day in machine learning.

2.2 The Likelihood Function

2.2.1 Definition and Construction

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} f(x;\theta)$ where $f$ is the probability mass function (discrete case) or probability density function (continuous case) indexed by the unknown parameter $\theta$. After observing the data $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$, the likelihood function is defined as follows.

Definition · Likelihood Function

The likelihood function $L: \Theta \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is defined as the joint density (or mass) of the data, viewed as a function of the parameter $\theta$ with the data held fixed: $$L(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i;\theta)$$ The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) is the parameter value that maximizes the likelihood: $$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta \in \Theta} L(\theta;\mathbf{x})$$

Intuition · Likelihood vs. Probability

A common confusion: the likelihood $L(\theta;\mathbf{x})$ is not the probability that $\theta$ equals some value. $\theta$ is a fixed unknown — it has no probability distribution in classical (frequentist) statistics. The likelihood is a function of $\theta$ that tells us how "compatible" each candidate value is with what we observed.

Formally: $L(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x};\theta)$, which is a probability over $\mathbf{x}$ for fixed $\theta$, but we then fix $\mathbf{x}$ and vary $\theta$. The same mathematical expression plays two roles depending on what you hold constant.

2.2.2 The Log-Likelihood and the Score Equation

The product structure of $L$ makes it analytically cumbersome. Since $\log$ is a strictly increasing function, maximizing $L$ is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood $\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta;\mathbf{x})$. The log converts the product into a sum — far more tractable.

Key Formula
$$\ell(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \log L(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \log f(x_i;\theta)$$
Figure 2.2. Likelihood $L(\mu)$ (left) and log-likelihood $\ell(\mu)$ (right) for the same Normal dataset ($n=15$). Both are maximized at $\hat\mu = \bar x$ (red dashed line). The log-likelihood is a sum — numerically stable, concave, and easy to differentiate. The gold shaded region marks the 95\% confidence interval implied by the curvature at the peak.

To find the MLE analytically, we differentiate and set the derivative to zero. The resulting equation is called the score equation (or likelihood equation).

Definition · Score Function and Score Equation

The score function is the derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to $\theta$: $$s(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\log f(x_i;\theta)$$ The score equation (or likelihood equation) is: $$s(\hat\theta;\mathbf{x}) = 0$$ Solutions to this equation are candidates for the MLE. We must verify that the solution is a maximum (not a minimum or saddle point) by checking the second derivative or by inspecting the likelihood directly. When the parameter space $\Theta$ has boundary points, the MLE may lie on the boundary, where the score equation need not hold.

Warning · Score Equation ≠ MLE

Solving the score equation gives a critical point of the log-likelihood, not necessarily the global maximum. Always verify: (1) check the second derivative (negative = local max), (2) compare to boundary values, and (3) if the log-likelihood is multimodal, numerical methods may converge to a local maximum. In practice, convex log-likelihoods (exponential family) guarantee a unique global maximum.

2.2.3 Worked Examples

Bernoulli Model

Figure 2.1. Bernoulli likelihood $L(p)=p^S(1-p)^{n-S}$ for three datasets. The peak always lands at $\hat p = S/n$ — the sample proportion. Larger $n$ produces a sharper, more concentrated curve, reflecting that the data are more informative about $p$.

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Bernoulli}(p)$, so $f(x;p) = p^x(1-p)^{1-x}$ for $x \in \{0,1\}$. The log-likelihood is: $$\ell(p) = \sum_{i=1}^n \log f(x_i;p) = \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl[x_i \log p + (1-x_i)\log(1-p)\bigr]$$ $$= \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)\log p + \left(n - \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)\log(1-p)$$ Let $S = \sum x_i$ be the number of successes. The score equation gives: $$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{S}{p} - \frac{n-S}{1-p} = 0$$ Solving: $S(1-p) = p(n-S)$, so $S - Sp = pn - pS$, and thus: $$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{S}{n} = \bar{X}}$$ The MLE of a Bernoulli proportion is the sample proportion — exactly what intuition suggests. The log-likelihood is strictly concave in $p$, confirming this is a global maximum.

Poisson Model

Let $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, so $f(x;\lambda) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x/x!$. The log-likelihood is: $$\ell(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^n\bigl[-\lambda + x_i\log\lambda - \log(x_i!)\bigr] = -n\lambda + \left(\sum x_i\right)\log\lambda + \text{const}$$ Score equation: $$\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda} = 0 \implies \hat\lambda_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{X}$$ Again the sample mean. Note: $\mathbb{E}[X] = \lambda$ for a Poisson, so the MoM gives the same answer here.

Normal Model — Both Parameters Unknown

Let $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The log-likelihood is: $$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\log\sigma^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i - \mu)^2$$

Taking partial derivatives and setting both to zero:

  • $\partial\ell/\partial\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \hat\mu_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{X}$
  • $\partial\ell/\partial\sigma^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \hat\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2$
Warning · MLE of $\sigma^2$ Is Biased

The MLE divides by $n$, not $n-1$. As we proved in Chapter 1, $\mathbb{E}[\hat\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2 \neq \sigma^2$. The MLE is biased for $\sigma^2$. This does not mean MLE is broken — it means that unbiasedness is not a defining property of MLE. The bias is $O(1/n)$ and vanishes asymptotically. For large samples, the distinction between dividing by $n$ and $n-1$ is negligible.

Figure 2.3. Normal log-likelihood $\ell(\mu)$ for increasing sample sizes. Each curve is normalized to zero at its peak. Larger $n$ produces a steeper parabola — the curvature equals $n/\sigma^2$, which is the Fisher information. The narrowing of the curve directly corresponds to a shrinking confidence interval: the MLE becomes more and more precise.

2.2.4 Regression versus ML Estimation

A natural question arises in the context of linear regression: when we fit $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$ by ordinary least squares (OLS), are we doing anything like MLE?

The answer is yes — under a specific distributional assumption.

Theorem · OLS = MLE under Gaussian Errors

Assume $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, so that $Y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i, \sigma^2)$. Then the MLE of $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$ is exactly the OLS estimator: $$(\hat\beta_0^{\text{MLE}}, \hat\beta_1^{\text{MLE}}) = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\beta_0, \beta_1}\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$

Proof sketch

The log-likelihood for the regression model is: $$\ell(\beta_0,\beta_1,\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$ Maximizing over $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$ for fixed $\sigma^2$ is equivalent to minimizing $\sum(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$ — which is exactly the OLS criterion. □

This equivalence is profound. OLS regression is not just a computational trick for fitting lines — it is statistically principled precisely because it corresponds to MLE under Gaussian errors. When errors are non-Gaussian (e.g., heavy-tailed), OLS is no longer the MLE and better alternatives exist (e.g., Laplace errors give MLE = least absolute deviations).

Data Science Application · Logistic Regression

When the response is binary ($Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$), the Gaussian error model is inappropriate. Logistic regression instead assumes $Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\pi_i)$ where $\pi_i = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)$ and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. The log-likelihood is: $$\ell(\boldsymbol\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl[y_i \log \pi_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\pi_i)\bigr] = -\text{Cross-Entropy}$$ Maximizing this log-likelihood is minimizing binary cross-entropy. There is no closed-form solution; gradient descent (or Newton's method) is used to find $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}_{\text{MLE}}$ numerically.

2.3 Properties of Estimators

We now have recipes for constructing estimators (MoM, MLE). But how do we know if an estimator is good? We need formal criteria. The three classical properties are unbiasedness, consistency, and efficiency. Understanding all three — and the tradeoffs between them — is essential for practical data science.

2.3.1 Unbiasedness

Definition · Unbiased Estimator

An estimator $\hat\theta_n$ is unbiased for $\theta$ if: $$\mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta_n] = \theta \quad \text{for all } \theta \in \Theta$$ The bias of an estimator is $\text{Bias}(\hat\theta_n) = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta_n] - \theta$. An estimator is asymptotically unbiased if $\text{Bias}(\hat\theta_n) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

Unbiasedness means the sampling distribution of $\hat\theta_n$ is centered at the truth. But "centered at the truth" does not mean "correct on every sample" — an unbiased estimator can be far wrong on any particular dataset. It just means that if you repeated the experiment many times, the average of all your estimates would converge to $\theta$.

Example · Unbiased vs. Biased Estimators of $\sigma^2$

From Chapter 1:

  • $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ is unbiased: $\mathbb{E}[S^2] = \sigma^2$
  • $\hat\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ is biased: $\mathbb{E}[\hat\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$

But note: $S$ (the sample standard deviation) is not unbiased for $\sigma$. Unbiasedness does not survive nonlinear transformations — a critical point we will revisit with the invariance principle.

Figure 2.4. Sampling distributions of the biased MLE $\hat\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$ (orange) and the unbiased $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$ (blue), from 20\,000 simulations with $n=10$ and $\sigma^2=4$. The unbiased estimator is centered exactly at the true value (red dashed). The MLE mean is shifted left to $(n-1)/n \times 4 = 3.6$. Both distributions narrow as $n \to \infty$.
Intuition · Is Unbiasedness Always Desirable?

Surprisingly, no. Unbiasedness is a local property (expectation), not a global one (all realizations). In high dimensions, the James–Stein estimator is biased for the mean of a multivariate normal but has strictly lower MSE than the unbiased sample mean. Ridge regression deliberately introduces bias to shrink coefficients — trading bias for dramatic variance reduction. The correct criterion is MSE, not bias alone.

2.3.2 Consistency

Definition · Consistent Estimator

An estimator $\hat\theta_n$ is consistent for $\theta$ if it converges in probability to $\theta$ as $n \to \infty$: $$\hat\theta_n \overset{p}{\to} \theta \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$ That is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$: $\mathbb{P}(|\hat\theta_n - \theta| > \varepsilon) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

Consistency is the minimum requirement for a reasonable estimator. If an estimator is not consistent, it will not converge to the truth no matter how much data you collect. Every statistician insists on consistency before considering other properties.

Theorem · Sufficient Condition for Consistency

If $\text{Bias}(\hat\theta_n) \to 0$ and $\text{Var}(\hat\theta_n) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, then $\hat\theta_n$ is consistent for $\theta$.

Proof

By Chebyshev's inequality: $\mathbb{P}(|\hat\theta_n - \theta| > \varepsilon) \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[(\hat\theta_n - \theta)^2]}{\varepsilon^2} = \frac{\text{MSE}(\hat\theta_n)}{\varepsilon^2}$. Since $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} \to 0$, the probability goes to zero. □

Example · Consistency of $\bar{X}$

$\bar{X}_n$ is unbiased for $\mu$ (so bias $= 0$ for all $n$) and $\text{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2/n \to 0$. Therefore $\bar{X}_n \overset{p}{\to} \mu$. This is nothing other than the Law of Large Numbers.

Warning · Consistent But Biased

The MLE $\hat\sigma^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ is biased for $\sigma^2$: the bias is $-\sigma^2/n$. But the bias $\to 0$ and the variance $\to 0$, so it is still consistent. This illustrates that unbiasedness and consistency are different properties. An estimator can be consistent but biased at every finite $n$, or unbiased but inconsistent (though this is rare in practice).

2.3.3 Efficiency and the Cramér–Rao Bound

Given that many different estimators might all be consistent, which should we prefer? Efficiency formalizes the notion of "getting the most information out of the data." The key insight is that there is a fundamental lower bound on how small the variance of any unbiased estimator can be.

Theorem · Cramér–Rao Lower Bound

Let $\hat\theta_n$ be any unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter $\theta$. Under regularity conditions (the support of $f(x;\theta)$ does not depend on $\theta$, and differentiation and integration can be interchanged), we have: $$\text{Var}(\hat\theta_n) \geq \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}$$ where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher information (defined in Section 2.5.1): $$I(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\theta\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log f(X;\theta)\right)^2\right] = -\mathbb{E}_\theta\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log f(X;\theta)\right]$$

Figure 2.6. Cramér–Rao lower bound (gold dashed) and estimator variances as a function of $n$ for a Bernoulli($p=0.4$) model. The sample proportion $\hat p = \bar X$ (blue) achieves the bound exactly — it is the UMVUE. No unbiased estimator can enter the forbidden zone below the gold curve. A hypothetical suboptimal estimator (gray dotted) lies strictly above.
Definition · Efficient Estimator

An unbiased estimator $\hat\theta_n$ is efficient if it achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound with equality: $$\text{Var}(\hat\theta_n) = \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}$$ An estimator is asymptotically efficient if its asymptotic variance (as $n \to \infty$) equals the Cramér–Rao bound. As we will see in Section 2.5, the MLE is asymptotically efficient under regularity conditions — one of its most powerful properties.

Example · Efficiency of $\bar{X}$ for a Normal Population

For $X_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, the Fisher information is $I(\mu) = 1/\sigma^2$. The Cramér–Rao bound is: $$\text{Var}(\hat\mu) \geq \frac{1}{n \cdot (1/\sigma^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$ The sample mean $\bar{X}$ achieves exactly $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \sigma^2/n$. Therefore $\bar{X}$ is the uniformly minimum variance unbiased estimator (UMVUE) for $\mu$ in the normal model.

2.3.4 The Bias–Variance Tradeoff in Estimation

We now return to the decomposition introduced in Section 2.1.1: $$\text{MSE}(\hat\theta_n) = \text{Bias}(\hat\theta_n)^2 + \text{Var}(\hat\theta_n)$$ This is not just a mathematical identity — it encodes a deep tension that appears throughout statistics and machine learning.

Intuition · Why There Is a Tradeoff

Reducing bias typically requires making an estimator more "responsive" to the data — more adaptive to local patterns. But adaptiveness increases sensitivity to noise, which inflates variance. Reducing variance typically requires some form of shrinkage or regularization — pulling estimates toward a fixed value — which introduces bias. You cannot minimize both simultaneously in general.

Consider the family of estimators $\hat\mu_c = c\bar{X}$ for $c \in (0, 1]$, used to estimate a population mean $\mu$:

  • Bias: $\mathbb{E}[c\bar{X}] - \mu = (c-1)\mu$. For $c < 1$, this is nonzero — $c\bar{X}$ is biased toward zero.
  • Variance: $\text{Var}(c\bar{X}) = c^2\sigma^2/n$. For $c < 1$, this is smaller than $\sigma^2/n$.
  • MSE: $(c-1)^2\mu^2 + c^2\sigma^2/n$. This can be smaller than the MSE of $\bar{X}$ (which is $\sigma^2/n$) for certain values of $c$.
Figure 2.5. Bias–variance tradeoff for the shrinkage estimator $\hat\mu_c = c\bar X$ ($\mu=2$, $\sigma^2=1$, $n=10$). Purple = total MSE; red dashed = Bias²; blue dashed = Variance. The optimal $c^* = 0.98$ (green dotted) achieves lower MSE than the unbiased $\bar X$ ($c=1$). The green shaded region marks where shrinkage outperforms the unbiased estimator.
Bias–Variance Decomposition
$$\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\bigr]}_{\text{MSE}} = \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \theta\bigr)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta])^2\bigr]}_{\text{Variance}}$$
Data Science Application · Ridge Regression

Ridge regression estimates $\boldsymbol\beta$ by minimizing the penalized least squares: $$\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}_{\text{ridge}} = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\boldsymbol\beta} \bigl\{\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 + \lambda\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2\bigr\}$$ The ridge estimator is biased: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}_{\text{ridge}}] \neq \boldsymbol\beta$ in general. But it has substantially lower variance than OLS when predictors are correlated (multicollinearity), and its total MSE can be far lower. Ridge regression is the canonical example of beneficially biased estimation — trading bias for variance in a way that reduces prediction error.

The bias–variance tradeoff is not unique to regression. It appears in:

  • k-nearest neighbors: small $k$ = low bias, high variance; large $k$ = high bias, low variance
  • Kernel density estimation: small bandwidth = low bias, high variance; large bandwidth = high bias, low variance
  • Neural networks: more parameters = lower bias on training data; regularization (dropout, weight decay) reduces overfitting (variance) at cost of bias

2.4 Properties of ML Estimates

The MLE has several remarkable properties that make it the estimator of choice in most parametric modeling problems. Two of the most practically useful are the invariance principle and the ability to handle multivariate discrete models like the multinomial.

2.4.1 The Invariance Principle

Theorem · Invariance of MLE

If $\hat\theta$ is the MLE of $\theta$, and $g: \Theta \to \mathbb{R}$ is any function of $\theta$, then the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is $g(\hat\theta)$: $$\widehat{g(\theta)}_{\text{MLE}} = g(\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}})$$ This holds even when $g$ is not invertible.

Proof (invertible case)

Let $\phi = g(\theta)$. Then $\theta = g^{-1}(\phi)$ and: $$L^*(\phi) = L(g^{-1}(\phi))$$ The maximum of $L^*$ over $\phi$ is achieved at $\phi^* = g(\hat\theta)$, since $L^*(g(\hat\theta)) = L(\hat\theta) = \max_\theta L(\theta)$. □

The invariance principle is extremely useful in practice because it means you never have to re-derive an MLE for a transformed parameter. You simply plug the MLE into the transformation.

Examples of the Invariance Principle
  • For $X_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$: $\hat\mu_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{X}$, so the MLE of the coefficient of variation $\mu/\sigma$ is $\bar{X}/\hat\sigma_{\text{MLE}}$.
  • For $X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$: $\hat p_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{X}$, so the MLE of the odds $p/(1-p)$ is $\bar{X}/(1-\bar{X})$.
  • For $X_i \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda)$: $\hat\lambda_{\text{MLE}} = 1/\bar{X}$, so the MLE of the mean $1/\lambda$ is $\bar{X}$.
  • For $X_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$: $\hat\lambda_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{X}$, so the MLE of $\mathbb{P}(X=0) = e^{-\lambda}$ is $e^{-\bar{X}}$.
Warning · Invariance Does Not Preserve Unbiasedness

While $\hat\mu_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{X}$ is unbiased for $\mu$, the MLE of $\mu^2$ is $\bar{X}^2$, which is biased (by Jensen's inequality, $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^2] = \mu^2 + \sigma^2/n > \mu^2$). The invariance principle gives you the MLE of a transformed parameter, but the resulting estimator need not be unbiased. This is expected — unbiasedness is not a property of MLE.

2.4.2 MLE for Multinomial Cell Probabilities

The multinomial model generalizes the Bernoulli to $k \geq 2$ categories. Suppose we observe $n$ independent trials, where each trial falls into category $j$ with probability $p_j$, $j = 1, \ldots, k$, with $\sum_{j=1}^k p_j = 1$. Let $n_j$ = number of observations in category $j$, so $\sum n_j = n$.

Theorem · MLE for Multinomial Probabilities

For the multinomial model with $n$ observations and $k$ categories with probabilities $(p_1, \ldots, p_k)$ subject to $\sum p_j = 1$, the MLE is: $$\hat p_j = \frac{n_j}{n}, \quad j = 1, \ldots, k$$ That is, the MLE of each cell probability is the observed relative frequency in that category.

Derivation

The likelihood is proportional to the multinomial PMF: $$L(p_1,\ldots,p_k) \propto \prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{n_j}$$ The log-likelihood is $\ell = \sum_{j=1}^k n_j \log p_j$. We maximize subject to the constraint $\sum p_j = 1$ using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial p_j}\left(\sum_{j=1}^k n_j\log p_j - \lambda\sum_{j=1}^k p_j\right) = \frac{n_j}{p_j} - \lambda = 0$$ So $p_j = n_j/\lambda$. Using $\sum p_j = 1$: $\lambda = \sum n_j = n$. Thus $\hat p_j = n_j/n$. □

This result underpins a vast range of applications in data science:

  • Language models: The MLE of the probability of a word given its context is the relative frequency of that word following that context in the training corpus — the basis of n-gram language models.
  • Contingency tables: When we have two categorical variables and test for independence, the expected cell counts under independence are obtained by plugging in the MLEs $\hat p_j = n_j/n$ for marginal probabilities.
  • Naive Bayes classifiers: Class-conditional probabilities are estimated by relative frequencies — MLEs of multinomial parameters.
Example · Estimating Category Probabilities

A streaming service observes that out of $n = 10{,}000$ user sessions, users watched: Drama (3,200), Comedy (2,500), Documentary (1,800), Action (2,000), Other (500). The MLE of the probability of each genre is $\hat p = (0.32, 0.25, 0.18, 0.20, 0.05)$. By invariance, the MLE of the probability that a user watches either Drama or Comedy is $\hat p_\text{Drama} + \hat p_\text{Comedy} = 0.57$.

2.5 Large-Sample Theory for MLE

At a finite sample size $n$, the MLE is defined and can be computed, but its exact sampling distribution is often intractable. The power of large-sample (asymptotic) theory is that the MLE has a simple, universal limiting distribution under mild conditions — regardless of the underlying model. This gives us a practical way to quantify uncertainty and construct confidence intervals for any MLE.

2.5.1 Fisher Information

The Fisher information measures how much information the data carry about the parameter $\theta$. It is the expected curvature of the log-likelihood — how sharply the log-likelihood peaks near its maximum.

Definition · Fisher Information

For a single observation $X \sim f(x;\theta)$, the Fisher information is: $$I(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\theta\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log f(X;\theta)\right)^2\right]$$ Under regularity conditions (allowing interchange of differentiation and integration): $$I(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_\theta\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}\log f(X;\theta)\right]$$ For $n$ i.i.d. observations, the total Fisher information is $I_n(\theta) = nI(\theta)$.

Intuition · Curvature and Information

If the log-likelihood is sharply curved (large second derivative in magnitude), then nearby values of $\theta$ produce very different likelihoods — the data strongly discriminate between candidate parameter values. This means the data are highly informative. If the log-likelihood is flat, many values of $\theta$ are nearly as plausible as $\hat\theta$, meaning the data say little about $\theta$. Fisher information = curvature = informativeness.

Figure 2.7. Fisher information as log-likelihood curvature. Each curve shows the normalized log-likelihood $\ell(\mu) - \ell(\hat\mu)$ for different sample sizes. The curvature at the peak equals $nI(\theta) = n/\sigma^2$ — sharper peaks mean more information. The double-headed arrows indicate the 95\% confidence interval width $\propto 1/\sqrt{nI(\theta)}$: as $n$ quadruples, the CI width halves.
Example · Fisher Information for Common Models
ModelParameter$I(\theta)$
$\text{Bernoulli}(p)$$p$$\frac{1}{p(1-p)}$
$\text{Poisson}(\lambda)$$\lambda$$\frac{1}{\lambda}$
$\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$, $\sigma^2$ known$\mu$$\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$
$\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$, $\mu$ known$\sigma^2$$\frac{1}{2\sigma^4}$
$\text{Exponential}(\lambda)$$\lambda$$\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$

2.5.2 Asymptotic Normality of the MLE

The following theorem is the cornerstone of large-sample inference. It tells us that the MLE, regardless of the underlying model, is approximately normally distributed for large $n$ — with mean $\theta$ (asymptotically unbiased) and variance equal to the Cramér–Rao lower bound (asymptotically efficient).

Theorem · Asymptotic Normality and Efficiency of MLE

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} f(x;\theta_0)$ where $\theta_0$ is the true parameter. Under regularity conditions, the MLE $\hat\theta_n$ satisfies: $$\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta_n - \theta_0) \overset{d}{\to} \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \frac{1}{I(\theta_0)}\right)$$ Equivalently, for large $n$: $$\hat\theta_n \overset{\cdot}{\sim} \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta_0,\, \frac{1}{nI(\theta_0)}\right)$$ This means the MLE is: (1) consistent, (2) asymptotically unbiased, and (3) asymptotically efficient — it achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound in large samples.

Figure 2.8. Sampling distribution of the MLE $\hat p = \bar X$ for Bernoulli($p=0.35$) at four sample sizes (8\,000 simulations each). The gold curve is the theoretical asymptotic approximation $\mathcal{N}(p,\, p(1-p)/n)$. At $n=5$ the distribution is discrete and skewed; by $n=40$ the normal approximation is already very close; at $n=150$ it is nearly perfect.
Proof Sketch

Taylor-expand the score equation $\ell'(\hat\theta) = 0$ around $\theta_0$: $$0 = \ell'(\hat\theta) \approx \ell'(\theta_0) + \ell''(\theta_0)(\hat\theta - \theta_0)$$ Rearranging: $$\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta - \theta_0) \approx -\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\ell'(\theta_0)}{\frac{1}{n}\ell''(\theta_0)}$$ By the CLT, $\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\ell'(\theta_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum s(x_i;\theta_0) \overset{d}{\to} \mathcal{N}(0, I(\theta_0))$ (since the score has mean 0 and variance $I(\theta_0)$). By the LLN, $\frac{1}{n}\ell''(\theta_0) \overset{p}{\to} -I(\theta_0)$. Slutsky's theorem gives the result. □

Intuition · Why MLE Is Asymptotically Efficient

The CLT "funnels" the variability of the MLE down to $1/\sqrt{nI(\theta)}$. This is precisely the Cramér–Rao lower bound: no unbiased estimator can do better in large samples. The MLE uses the score function — which has variance exactly $I(\theta)$ — as its core building block. Roughly speaking, the MLE is the best unbiased estimator because it is built from the score, which is the most informative single-dimensional summary of a single observation.

2.5.3 Confidence Intervals from MLE

Asymptotic normality immediately gives us a practical method for constructing confidence intervals for any parameter estimated by MLE. The key steps are: (1) compute $\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}}$, (2) estimate the standard error using the observed or expected Fisher information, (3) invoke the normal approximation.

Definition · Observed and Expected Fisher Information

In practice, $I(\theta_0)$ is unknown. We estimate it in two ways:

  • Expected Fisher information: $\hat I = I(\hat\theta)$ — plug the MLE into the formula for $I(\theta)$.
  • Observed Fisher information: $\hat I = -\frac{1}{n}\ell''(\hat\theta)$ — use the curvature of the observed log-likelihood at $\hat\theta$. This is preferred in practice.
Either gives a consistent estimator of $I(\theta_0)$ by the continuous mapping theorem.

MLE Confidence Interval
$$\hat\theta \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat\theta), \qquad \text{where} \quad \widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat\theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n\hat I}}$$

This gives an approximate $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\theta$. For 95% intervals, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

Example · Confidence Interval for a Bernoulli Proportion

For $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Bernoulli}(p)$: $\hat p = \bar X$, $I(p) = 1/[p(1-p)]$. Estimated SE: $$\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat p) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}$$ The 95% CI is $\hat p \pm 1.96\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$ — the Wald confidence interval. In a survey of $n = 500$ voters, 260 plan to vote yes. Then $\hat p = 0.52$, $\widehat{\text{SE}} = \sqrt{0.52 \cdot 0.48/500} = 0.0223$, and the 95% CI is $(0.476, 0.564)$. We cannot rule out a majority for "no" at this sample size.

Figure 2.9. Sixty Wald 95\% confidence intervals for $p=0.4$ ($n=30$), each from an independent simulated sample. Green bars cover the true value (dashed vertical line); red bars miss. The empirical coverage is close to 95\%. The intervals vary in width because the SE depends on $\hat p$, which differs across samples.
Example · Confidence Interval for a Poisson Rate

A data center observes $n = 30$ days, with $\sum x_i = 270$ server failures. The MLE is $\hat\lambda = 270/30 = 9$ failures/day. Fisher information for Poisson: $I(\lambda) = 1/\lambda$, so $\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat\lambda) = \sqrt{\lambda/n} \approx \sqrt{9/30} = 0.548$. The 95% CI is $9 \pm 1.96 \times 0.548 = (7.93, 10.07)$ failures/day.

Data Science Application · Profile Likelihood Intervals

When the model has multiple parameters $\theta = (\psi, \nu)$ and we care only about $\psi$ (the parameter of interest) with $\nu$ as a nuisance parameter, we can use the profile log-likelihood: $$\ell_p(\psi) = \max_\nu \ell(\psi, \nu)$$ A confidence interval for $\psi$ based on the profile likelihood often has better coverage than the Wald interval in small samples and is invariant under reparametrization. Many modern statistical software packages (R's confint for GLMs, Python's statsmodels) use profile likelihood intervals by default.

Theorem · Likelihood Ratio Interval

An alternative to the Wald interval is the likelihood ratio confidence set: $$C_n = \left\{\theta : 2\left[\ell(\hat\theta) - \ell(\theta)\right] \leq \chi^2_{\alpha, 1}\right\}$$ where $\chi^2_{\alpha, 1}$ is the upper $\alpha$ quantile of the $\chi^2(1)$ distribution. By Wilks' theorem, this set has asymptotic coverage $(1-\alpha)$. Unlike the Wald interval, it is automatically constrained to the valid parameter space (e.g., $p \in [0,1]$) and tends to have better finite-sample coverage.

✦ Chapter Summary

This chapter developed the theory and practice of Maximum Likelihood Estimation from first principles. The central thread: given a parametric model $f(x;\theta)$ and observed data, MLE finds the parameter value that makes the data most probable. This simple principle, when made precise, yields a framework with extraordinary theoretical and practical reach.

Method of Moments

Equate sample moments $\hat\mu_k = n^{-1}\sum X_i^k$ to population moments $\mu_k(\theta)$, solve for $\theta$. Simple and often closed-form, but ignores distributional shape. Can produce biased estimators. Best used when MLE lacks a closed form.

Likelihood Function

$L(\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i;\theta)$ — the joint density of the data as a function of $\theta$. Maximizing $L$ (equivalently, maximizing the log-likelihood $\ell = \sum \log f$) gives the MLE. Solve the score equation $\ell'(\hat\theta) = 0$, verify it is a maximum.

MLE = OLS Under Gaussian Errors

For regression with $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$, MLE of $\boldsymbol\beta$ equals the OLS estimator. For logistic regression (Bernoulli $Y$), MLE corresponds to minimizing binary cross-entropy. MLE unifies and justifies the standard loss functions of ML.

Unbiasedness

$\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] = \theta$ for all $\theta$. MLE need not be unbiased ($\hat\sigma^2_\text{MLE}$ divides by $n$, not $n-1$). Unbiasedness is desirable but not required — the relevant criterion is MSE = Bias$^2$ + Variance, not bias alone.

Consistency

$\hat\theta_n \overset{p}{\to} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$ — the estimator converges to the truth with more data. The MLE is consistent under mild regularity conditions. Consistency is the minimum requirement for any practical estimator.

Efficiency & Cramér–Rao

No unbiased estimator can have variance below $1/[nI(\theta)]$ (the Cramér–Rao bound). The MLE achieves this bound asymptotically — it is asymptotically efficient. The Fisher information $I(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[(s')^2] = -\mathbb{E}[\ell'']$ measures data informativeness.

Bias–Variance Tradeoff

$\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}$. Reducing bias inflates variance and vice versa. Ridge regression, shrinkage estimators, and regularization all intentionally introduce bias to achieve lower total MSE. Appears universally across statistics and ML.

Invariance Principle

If $\hat\theta$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then $g(\hat\theta)$ is the MLE of $g(\theta)$ for any function $g$. Enormously practical: derive MLE once for $\theta$, then transform freely. Does not preserve unbiasedness. Unique to MLE — not shared by MoM.

Multinomial MLE

$\hat p_j = n_j/n$ — observed relative frequencies. Underpins naive Bayes, n-gram language models, chi-squared tests, and contingency table analysis. By invariance, any function of cell probabilities is estimated by plugging in $\hat p_j$.

Asymptotic Normality & CI

$\hat\theta_n \overset{\cdot}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\theta_0, 1/[nI(\theta_0)])$ for large $n$. Yields Wald CI: $\hat\theta \pm z_{\alpha/2}/\sqrt{n\hat I}$. Likelihood ratio intervals (Wilks' theorem) have better finite-sample coverage. All major statistical software computes these automatically.

Exercises

Exercise 2.1 · Deriving MLEs
MLE for the Exponential and Uniform Distributions

(a) Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Exponential}(\lambda)$ with density $f(x;\lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x > 0$. Derive the MLE $\hat\lambda$ and verify it is a maximum. Is $\hat\lambda$ unbiased? If not, find the bias and propose a bias-corrected estimator. (b) Let $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Uniform}(0,\theta)$. The log-likelihood is non-differentiable at the MLE. Argue directly from the likelihood that $\hat\theta = X_{(n)} = \max_i X_i$. Show that $\hat\theta$ is consistent but biased. Find an unbiased estimator of $\theta$ based on $X_{(n)}$. (c) Why does the Cramér–Rao bound not apply to the MLE for the Uniform distribution? (Hint: consider the regularity conditions.)

Exercise 2.2 · Method of Moments vs. MLE
Beta Distribution Estimation

Let $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with mean $\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$ and variance $\alpha\beta/[(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)]$. (a) Derive the method of moments estimators $\hat\alpha^{\text{MoM}}$ and $\hat\beta^{\text{MoM}}$ in terms of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ and sample variance $\hat\sigma^2$. (b) The log-likelihood for the Beta distribution involves the digamma function $\psi(\cdot)$, so the MLE has no closed form. Write the score equations. (c) Using the data $\{0.2, 0.7, 0.4, 0.85, 0.55, 0.3, 0.65\}$, compute the MoM estimates. (d) Discuss which estimator you would prefer in a setting where $n$ is very small vs. very large, and why.

Exercise 2.3 · Bias, Variance, and the MSE Tradeoff
Shrinkage Estimation of a Mean

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known. Consider the family of estimators $\hat\mu_c = c\bar{X}$ for $c \in (0, 1]$. (a) Compute $\text{Bias}(\hat\mu_c)$, $\text{Var}(\hat\mu_c)$, and $\text{MSE}(\hat\mu_c)$ as functions of $c$, $\mu$, $\sigma^2$, and $n$. (b) Find the value of $c^*$ that minimizes MSE. Express $c^*$ in terms of $\mu$, $\sigma^2$, and $n$. (c) Is $\hat\mu_{c^*}$ a practical estimator? Why or why not? (d) For what values of $(\mu, \sigma^2, n)$ does $\hat\mu_{c^*}$ have strictly lower MSE than $\bar{X}$? What does this say about the value of shrinkage when $\mu \approx 0$?

Exercise 2.4 · Fisher Information and Efficiency
Information in a Geometric Model

Let $X_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Geometric}(p)$ with PMF $f(x;p) = (1-p)^{x-1}p$ for $x = 1, 2, \ldots$ (a) Derive the MLE $\hat p$ and show that $\hat p = 1/\bar{X}$. Is $\hat p$ unbiased? (b) Compute the Fisher information $I(p)$. (c) Compute $\text{Var}(\hat p)$ using the delta method: if $\hat p = g(\bar{X})$ and $g(x) = 1/x$, then approximately $\text{Var}(\hat p) \approx [g'(\mu)]^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\bar{X})$. Compare $\text{Var}(\hat p)$ to the Cramér–Rao bound $1/(nI(p))$. Is $\hat p$ asymptotically efficient? (d) Construct an approximate 95% CI for $p$ from a sample of $n = 100$ geometric observations with $\bar{X} = 4.2$.

Exercise 2.5 · Multinomial MLE and Contingency Tables
Independence Testing via MLEs

An e-commerce platform records 5,000 user sessions by device (Mobile, Desktop) and action (Purchase, Browse, Bounce). The observed counts are: Mobile: 800 purchases, 1,200 browse, 600 bounce; Desktop: 400 purchases, 1,200 browse, 800 browse. (a) Compute the MLE of each of the 6 joint cell probabilities $p_{ij}$. (b) Under the independence model $p_{ij} = p_{i\cdot}p_{\cdot j}$, compute the MLE of the marginal probabilities $p_{i\cdot}$ and $p_{\cdot j}$. (c) Under independence, compute the expected cell counts $E_{ij} = n\hat p_{i\cdot}\hat p_{\cdot j}$. (d) By the invariance principle, the MLE of the log-odds ratio $\log\frac{p_{11}p_{22}}{p_{12}p_{21}}$ is obtained by plugging in $\hat p_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$. Compute this estimate and interpret its magnitude.

Chapter 2  ·  Statistical Inference  ·  Data Science Foundations
